revision.comment: 1 revision imported: making drafts attribute to me
revision.text:
'''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan}}), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制}}), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}, it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|ja|官僚}}. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}} Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan}}), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|ja|尚書省}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|ja|門下省}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}, it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官}} (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官}} (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官}} (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典}} (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table}}
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.
== Notes == {{Notelist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} * {{ill|Japanese government structure|ja|日本の官制}} * {{ill|Rank and Title in Japan|ja|官位}} * {{ill|Four-Rank Bureaucracy in China|ja|四等官}} * {{ill|Military ranks and insignia|ja|軍隊における階級呼称一覧}}
== Bibliography == * Wada Hidematsu, *New Edition: Explanation of Official Positions*, Kodansha, 1983. * Tonami Mamoru, *Tang Administration and Bureaucracy*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1998. * Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, *Essential Japanese History*, 2006. * Sato Zentoshi, *The Four-Rank System in Ancient Japan*, *Historical Journal*, 2007.
== External Links == * [http://www.sol.dti.ne.jp/~hiromi/kansei/ Bureaucratic Overview]

Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}, it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|ja|官僚}}. {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}} Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|ja|尚書省}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|ja|門下省}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan}}), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制}}), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}, it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|ja|官僚}}. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}} Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan}}), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|ja|尚書省}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|ja|門下省}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}, it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官}} (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官}} (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官}} (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典}} (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table}}
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.
== Notes == {{Notelist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} * {{ill|Japanese government structure|ja|日本の官制}} * {{ill|Rank and Title in Japan|ja|官位}} * {{ill|Four-Rank Bureaucracy in China|ja|四等官}} * {{ill|Military ranks and insignia|ja|軍隊における階級呼称一覧}}
== Bibliography == * Wada Hidematsu, *New Edition: Explanation of Official Positions*, Kodansha, 1983. * Tonami Mamoru, *Tang Administration and Bureaucracy*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1998. * Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, *Essential Japanese History*, 2006. * Sato Zentoshi, *The Four-Rank System in Ancient Japan*, *Historical Journal*, 2007.
== External Links == * [http://www.sol.dti.ne.jp/~hiromi/kansei/ Bureaucratic Overview]

'''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan}}), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制}}), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}, it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|ja|官僚}}. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}} Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan}}), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|ja|尚書省}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|ja|門下省}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}, it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官}} (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官}} (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官}} (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典}} (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table}}
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

pages]][[category{Four-Tier Officials]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[ja:四等官]] [[de:Shitōkan]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

'''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan}}), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制}}), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}, it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|ja|官僚}}. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}} Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan}}), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|ja|尚書省}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|ja|門下省}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}, it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官}} (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官}} (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官}} (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典}} (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table}}
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|ja|尚書省}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|ja|門下省}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}

pages]][[Category:Four-Tier Officials]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Four-Tier Officials]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:四等官]] [[de:Shitōkan]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

'''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan}}), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制}}), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}, it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|ja|官僚}}. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}} Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan}}), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|ja|尚書省}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|ja|門下省}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}, it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官}} (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官}} (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官}} (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典}} (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table}}
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Source Wikipedia]] to [[Category:from everydodywiki with needed marking of original title]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan}}), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制}}), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}, it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|ja|官僚}}. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}} Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan}}), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|ja|尚書省}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|ja|門下省}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}, it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官}} (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官}} (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官}} (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典}} (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table}}
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.

This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|ja|尚書省}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|ja|門下省}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}, it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官}} (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官}} (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官}} (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典}} (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table}}
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan}}), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制}}), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}, it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|ja|官僚}}. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}} Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan}}), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|ja|尚書省}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|ja|門下省}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}, it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官}} (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官}} (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官}} (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典}} (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table}}
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan}}), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制}}), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}, it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|ja|官僚}}. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}} Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan}}), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|ja|尚書省}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|ja|門下省}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}, it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官}} (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官}} (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官}} (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典}} (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table}}
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

== References == {{Reflist}}

== External Links == * [http://www.sol.dti.ne.jp/~hiromi/kansei/ Bureaucratic Overview]

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=source: [[every:Shitōkan]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:52, 13 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan}}), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制}}), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}, it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|ja|官僚}}. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}} Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan}}), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|ja|尚書省}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|ja|門下省}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}, it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官}} (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官}} (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官}} (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典}} (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table}}
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|ja|尚書省}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|ja|門下省}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Source Everybodywiki]] to [[Category:From Everybodywiki with needed marking of original title]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=source: [[every:Shitōkan]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:52, 13 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan}}), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制}}), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}, it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|ja|官僚}}. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}} Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan}}), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|ja|尚書省}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|ja|門下省}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}, it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官}} (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官}} (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官}} (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典}} (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table}}
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.
== Notes == {{Notelist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} * {{ill|Japanese government structure|ja|日本の官制}} * {{ill|Rank and Title in Japan|ja|官位}} * {{ill|Four-Rank Bureaucracy in China|ja|四等官}} * {{ill|Military ranks and insignia|ja|軍隊における階級呼称一覧}}
== Bibliography == * Wada Hidematsu, *New Edition: Explanation of Official Positions*, Kodansha, 1983. * Tonami Mamoru, *Tang Administration and Bureaucracy*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1998. * Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, *Essential Japanese History*, 2006. * Sato Zentoshi, *The Four-Rank System in Ancient Japan*, *Historical Journal*, 2007.
== External Links == * [http://www.sol.dti.ne.jp/~hiromi/kansei/ Bureaucratic Overview]

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=source: [[every:Shitōkan]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:52, 13 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan}}), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制}}), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}, it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|ja|官僚}}. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}} Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan}}), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|ja|尚書省}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|ja|門下省}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}, it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官}} (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官}} (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官}} (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典}} (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table}}
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=source: [[every:Shitōkan]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:52, 13 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan}}), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制}}), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}, it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|ja|官僚}}. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}} Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan}}), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|ja|尚書省}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|ja|門下省}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}, it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官}} (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官}} (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官}} (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典}} (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table}}
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=source: [[every:Shitōkan]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:52, 13 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan}}), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制}}), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}, it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|ja|官僚}}. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}} Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan}}), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|ja|尚書省}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|ja|門下省}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}, it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官}} (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官}} (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官}} (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典}} (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table}}
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=source: [[every:Shitōkan]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:52, 13 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan}}), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制}}), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}, it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|ja|官僚}}. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}} Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan}}), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|ja|尚書省}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|ja|門下省}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}, it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官}} (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官}} (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官}} (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典}} (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table}}
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.
== Notes == {{Notelist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} * {{ill|Japanese government structure|ja|日本の官制}} * {{ill|Rank and Title in Japan|ja|官位}} * {{ill|Four-Rank Bureaucracy in China|ja|四等官}} * {{ill|Military ranks and insignia|ja|軍隊における階級呼称一覧}}
== Bibliography == * Wada Hidematsu, *New Edition: Explanation of Official Positions*, Kodansha, 1983. * Tonami Mamoru, *Tang Administration and Bureaucracy*, Chūō Kōronsha, 1998. * Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, *Essential Japanese History*, 2006. * Sato Zentoshi, *The Four-Rank System in Ancient Japan*, *Historical Journal*, 2007.

Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|ja|尚書省}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|ja|門下省}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}

In {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=source: [[every:Shitōkan]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:52, 13 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan}}), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制}}), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}, it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|ja|官僚}}. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}} Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan}}), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|ja|尚書省}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|ja|門下省}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}, it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官}} (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官}} (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官}} (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典}} (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table}}
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

[[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Four-Tier Officials]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Four-Tier Officials]][[Category:qq]] {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source {{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=source: [[every:Shitōkan]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:52, 13 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan}}), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制}}), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}, it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|ja|官僚}}. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}} Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan}}), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|ja|尚書省}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|ja|門下省}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}, it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官}} (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官}} (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官}} (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典}} (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table}}
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=source: [[every:Shitōkan]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:52, 13 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan}}), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制}}), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}, it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|ja|官僚}}. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}} Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan}}), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|ja|尚書省}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|ja|門下省}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}, it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官}} (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官}} (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官}} (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典}} (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table}}
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=source: [[every:Shitōkan]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:52, 13 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan}}), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制}}), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}, it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|ja|官僚}}. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}} Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan}}), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|ja|尚書省}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|ja|門下省}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}, it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官}} (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官}} (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官}} (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典}} (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table}}
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

[[Category:qq]][[sn:Four-Tier Officials]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Four-Tier Officials]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:qqqq]] {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=source: [[every:Shitōkan]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:52, 13 February 2025 (UTC)}} ''''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan}}), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制}}), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}, it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|ja|官僚}}. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}} Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan}}), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|ja|尚書省}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|ja|門下省}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}, it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官}} (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官}} (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官}} (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典}} (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table}}
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.
