''''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan}}), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan制}}), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]]. Originating in China's [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]], it served as the foundation of an elaborate [[:en:Bureaucrat|bureaucratic system]]. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
[[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang dynasty]] Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan}}), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-Rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third Rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second Rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner-stream officials|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in Rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | [[:en:Shangshu Sheng|Shangshu Sheng]] || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shangshu Ling|1=Shangshu Ling|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11039246}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Ministries|qq=}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | [[:en:Menxia Sheng|Menxia Sheng]] || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] and [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]], it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官}} (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官}} (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官}} (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典}} (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table}}
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In [[:en:Silla|Silla]], positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

''''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan}}), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan制}}), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system}}. Originating in China's {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō}}, it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|Bureaucrat|en|Bureaucrat|WD=Q572700|lt=bureaucratic system}}. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.

== Overview ==

The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.

== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==

{{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang dynasty}} Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan}}), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.

In Tang law:
- Fourth-Rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions.
- Adjudicators (third Rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers.
- Deputy officers (second Rank) reviewed and finalized decisions.
- Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.

This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>

{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner-stream officials|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in Rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).

; Tang Four-Rank Table
{| class="wikitable"
|-
! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer
|-
| {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|en|Shangshu Sheng|WD=Q698561|lt=Shangshu Sheng}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shangshu Ling|1=Shangshu Ling|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11039246}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi
|-
| {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Ministries|qq=}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi
|-
| {{ill|Menxia Sheng|en|Menxia Sheng|WD=Q10839649|lt=Menxia Sheng}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi
|}

== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==

Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code}}, it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官}} (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官}} (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官}} (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典}} (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."

{{Four-Rank Table}}

== Four-Rank System in Silla ==

In {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.

== Other Applications ==

During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

''''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan}}), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan制}}), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system}}. Originating in China's {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō}}, it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|Bureaucrat|en|Bureaucrat|WD=Q572700|lt=bureaucratic system}}. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.

== Overview ==

The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.

== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==

{{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang dynasty}} Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan}}), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.

In Tang law:
- Fourth-Rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions.
- Adjudicators (third Rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers.
- Deputy officers (second Rank) reviewed and finalized decisions.
- Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.

This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>

{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner-stream officials|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in Rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).

; Tang Four-Rank Table
{| class="wikitable"
|-
! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer
|-
| {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|en|Shangshu Sheng|WD=Q698561|lt=Shangshu Sheng}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shangshu Ling|1=Shangshu Ling|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11039246}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi
|-
| {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Ministries|qq=}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi
|-
| {{ill|Menxia Sheng|en|Menxia Sheng|WD=Q10839649|lt=Menxia Sheng}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi
|}

== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==

Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code}}, it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官}} (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官}} (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官}} (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典}} (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."

{{Four-Rank Table}}

== Four-Rank System in Silla ==

In {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.

== Other Applications ==

During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

''''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan}}), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan制}}), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system}}. Originating in China's {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō}}, it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|Bureaucrat|en|Bureaucrat|WD=Q572700|lt=bureaucratic system}}. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.

== Overview ==

The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.

== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==

{{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang dynasty}} Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan}}), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.

In Tang law:
- Fourth-Rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions.
- Adjudicators (third Rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers.
- Deputy officers (second Rank) reviewed and finalized decisions.
- Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.

This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>

{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner-stream officials|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in Rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).

; Tang Four-Rank Table
{| class="wikitable"
|-
! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer
|-
| {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|en|Shangshu Sheng|WD=Q698561|lt=Shangshu Sheng}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shangshu Ling|1=Shangshu Ling|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11039246}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi
|-
| {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Ministries|qq=}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi
|-
| {{ill|Menxia Sheng|en|Menxia Sheng|WD=Q10839649|lt=Menxia Sheng}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi
|}

== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==

Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code}}, it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官}} (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官}} (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官}} (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典}} (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."

{{Four-Rank Table}}

== Four-Rank System in Silla ==

In {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.

== Other Applications ==

During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

''''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan}}), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan制}}), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō}}. Originating in China's {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō|1=Traditional Chinese law}}, it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|Bureaucrat|en|Bureaucrat|WD=Q572700|lt=bureaucratic system|1=Bureaucrat}}. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.

== Overview ==

The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.

== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==

{{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang dynasty|1=Tang dynasty}} Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan}}), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.

In Tang law:
- Fourth-Rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions.
- Adjudicators (third Rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers.
- Deputy officers (second Rank) reviewed and finalized decisions.
- Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.

This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>

{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner-stream officials|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in Rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).

; Tang Four-Rank Table
{| class="wikitable"
|-
! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer
|-
| {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|en|Shangshu Sheng|WD=Q698561|lt=Shangshu Sheng|1=Shangshu Sheng}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shangshu Ling|1=Q11039246|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11039246}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi
|-
| {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Ministries|qq=}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi
|-
| {{ill|Menxia Sheng|en|Menxia Sheng|WD=Q10839649|lt=Menxia Sheng|1=Menxia Sheng}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi
|}

== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==

Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Code}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code|1=Yōrō Code}}, it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官}} (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官}} (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官}} (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典}} (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."

{{Four-Rank Table}}

== Four-Rank System in Silla ==

In {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.

== Other Applications ==

During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

In {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.

== Other Applications ==

During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

''''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan}}), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan制}}), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō}}. Originating in China's {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō|1=Traditional Chinese law}}, it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|Bureaucrat|en|Bureaucrat|WD=Q572700|lt=bureaucratic system|1=Bureaucrat}}. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.

== Overview ==

The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.

== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==

{{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang dynasty|1=Tang dynasty}} Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan}}), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.

In Tang law:
- Fourth-Rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions.
- Adjudicators (third Rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers.
- Deputy officers (second Rank) reviewed and finalized decisions.
- Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.

This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>

{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner-stream officials|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in Rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).

; Tang Four-Rank Table
{| class="wikitable"
|-
! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer
|-
| {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|en|Shangshu Sheng|WD=Q698561|lt=Shangshu Sheng|1=Shangshu Sheng}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shangshu Ling|1=Q11039246|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11039246}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi
|-
| {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Ministries|qq=}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi
|-
| {{ill|Menxia Sheng|en|Menxia Sheng|WD=Q10839649|lt=Menxia Sheng|1=Menxia Sheng}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi
|}

== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==

Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Code}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code|1=Yōrō Code}}, it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官}} (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官}} (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官}} (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典}} (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."

{{Four-Rank Table}}

== Four-Rank System in Silla ==

In {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.

== Other Applications ==

During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

- Deputy officers (second Rank) reviewed and finalized decisions.
- Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.

This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>

{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner-stream officials|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in Rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).

; Tang Four-Rank Table

|-
! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer
|-
| {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|en|Shangshu Sheng|WD=Q698561|lt=Shangshu Sheng|1=Shangshu Sheng}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shangshu Ling|1=Q11039246|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11039246}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi

== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==

Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Code}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code|1=Yōrō Code}}, it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官}} (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官}} (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官}} (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典}} (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."

{{Four-Rank Table}}

== Four-Rank System in Silla ==

In {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.

== Other Applications ==

During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.
